skill.name: blitz
skill.description: Coordinate parallel GitHub issue work with git worktrees, Claude agents, review gates, and sequential pull request merges.
skill.summary: Runs structured multi-issue coding sprints with isolated worktrees and agent review loops.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command patterns because the skill is a workflow guide for git, GitHub CLI, and parallel agents. Review found no confirmed malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or real C2 behavior. The remaining risk is operational: users may close issues, merge pull requests, force-push branches, delete branches, and remove worktrees if they follow the workflow.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Mutating GitHub and Git Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to close issues, create review comments, squash-merge pull requests, rebase branches, and force-push updates. These are legitimate for the workflow, but they can change repository history or project state if used without human review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and perform write actions against GitHub or git history. The context is legitimate automation guidance, so this is operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Cleanup Commands Require Care
medium_findings.description: The skill includes branch deletion, worktree removal, worktree pruning, and a reset command in troubleshooting guidance. These commands can discard local work when used on the wrong branch or directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly recommends cleanup commands that can remove branches or reset local changes. The guidance is bounded to git maintenance, but misuse can cause data loss.

medium_findings.title: Parallel Agent Workflow Can Amplify Mistakes
medium_findings.description: The workflow delegates multiple issues to parallel agents and tells them to create pull requests after implementation and self-review. This is useful, but it increases review burden and can multiply bad changes if issue requirements are incomplete.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The delegation and merge gates are clearly described. The risk depends on user oversight and repository protections, so confidence is high for operational concern but not for malicious intent.

low_findings.title: C2 Static Matches Are Mermaid Node Labels
low_findings.description: The C2 matches occur inside a Mermaid diagram example with node names such as cleanup and reconnect. No command-and-control behavior or network beaconing evidence was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched text is inside a diagram code block and describes WebSocket reconnection flow labels. There is no executable network client or external host reference in that context.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Static Matches Are Unsupported
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not align with cryptographic code in the cited areas. The reviewed lines contain skill metadata, tool names, branch naming, or issue context instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hashing, encryption, or password handling in the cited lines. These alerts are false positives from text content rather than crypto usage.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory Access Is Worktree Isolation
low_findings.description: The hidden path references point to the .worktrees directory used for git worktree isolation. This is normal for the workflow and not hidden secret access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The directory name is consistently used as a workspace container for git worktrees. No evidence shows access to hidden credential files or sensitive configuration files.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is built around shell commands for gh, git, ps, grep, and kill. Commands are examples for an operator or agent, not code executed by the skill package itself.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command blocks are explicit and numerous. The file is markdown guidance, so the pattern is dangerous only when an agent follows it with repository permissions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Force Push and Branch Deletion
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes force-with-lease pushes and local branch deletion during merge and cleanup. These operations should run only after review and on the intended branch.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These exact git operations are present in the guidance and can alter branch state. The instructions do include safer force-with-lease usage, reducing severity.

content.value_statement: Multi-issue backlogs are slow when every fix waits on one working tree. Blitz provides a structured worktree and agent workflow for parallel pull request delivery.
actual_capabilities: Guides triage of open GitHub issues before starting parallel work.
Creates one git worktree and branch per independent issue.
Provides agent delegation prompts with issue requirements and review gates.
Defines pull request creation, review posting, and issue linking checks.
Runs a final coverage gate before sequential squash merges.
Documents cleanup steps for worktrees, branches, and local state.
limitations: Requires authenticated GitHub CLI access and git worktree support.
Assumes issues are independent enough for parallel implementation.
Does not replace human review for risky repository operations.
Can cause data loss if cleanup commands target the wrong branch.

use_cases.title: Batch Fix Independent Issues
use_cases.description: Split several clear GitHub issues into isolated worktrees and assign each one to a separate agent.

use_cases.title: Run a Review-Gated Sprint
use_cases.description: Use coverage tables and self-review loops before merging agent-generated pull requests.

use_cases.title: Clean Up After Parallel Work
use_cases.description: Remove worktrees, delete merged branches, and verify local main after a multi-PR sprint.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Small Blitz
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a few independent issues.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use blitz for issues #12, #15, and #18. Triage them, create worktrees, and prepare agent prompts before any merge.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Agent Delegation
prompt_templates.scenario: Build complete prompts for parallel agents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use blitz to extract all requirements from issues #21 through #24 and draft one worktree-specific prompt for each agent.

prompt_templates.title: Review Parallel Pull Requests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use blitz to inspect the open pull requests from this sprint and verify issue coverage, review status, and closing issue references.

prompt_templates.title: Recover a Conflicted Sprint
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with rebases and cleanup.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use blitz to order these pull requests by dependency, rebase each branch onto main, resolve conflicts, and merge only after final coverage gates pass.

output_examples.input: Use blitz on four independent bug issues.
output_examples.output: A sprint plan with issue triage, four worktree names, branch names, and separate agent assignments.
A coverage assessment showing implemented requirements, missing items, review score, issue link status, and a merge or resume decision.

output_examples.input: Clean up after all sprint pull requests merged.
output_examples.output: A cleanup checklist covering worktree removal, local branch deletion, main branch sync, and clean-state verification.

best_practices: Extract every issue requirement before delegating work to agents.
Use one isolated worktree and branch for each independent issue.
Verify coverage, tests, and issue links before every merge.
anti_patterns: Do not run a blitz on tightly coupled issues without dependency ordering.
Do not merge agent pull requests based only on self-review text.
Do not run cleanup commands until branch and worktree targets are confirmed.

faq.question: What does Blitz help automate?
faq.answer: It structures parallel issue work with git worktrees, agent prompts, pull requests, review gates, and cleanup steps.

faq.question: Does it execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides workflow instructions and command examples for the assistant or user to run deliberately.

faq.question: Which tools are required?
faq.answer: It expects GitHub CLI authentication, git worktree support, and an AI tool that can delegate parallel agent tasks.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The workflow uses powerful git and GitHub commands that can change repository state when followed.

faq.question: Can Codex use this workflow?
faq.answer: Yes. Codex can adapt the worktree, triage, review, and merge process when repository permissions are available.

faq.question: When should I avoid this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid it when issues are unclear, highly coupled, or require manual product decisions before implementation.
